name: CHEMISTRY (Theory)
question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/4/2 (set-2)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 11 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into three Sections - A, B and C.
  3. Section A - Questions no. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section B - Questions no. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section C - Question no. 12 is case based question, carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is not allowed.

SECTION A
1.

In a plot of {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} against the square root of concentration (C1/2) for strong and weak electrolyte, the value of limiting molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte cannot be obtained graphically. Suggest a way to obtain this value. Also state the related law, if any.

 2
2.

Write reasons for the following statements:

  1. Benzoic acid does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  2. Oxidation of aldehydes is easier than that of ketones.
 2
3.

Give reasons for the following statements: (Any two)

  1. Benzaldehyde is less reactive than propanal in nucleophilic addition reactions.
  2. Carboxylic acids do not give reactions of carbonyl group.
  3. 4-nitrobenzoic acid is a stronger acid than benzoic acid.
 2

SECTION B
4.(a)
  1. Silver atom has completely filled d-orbitals in its ground state, it is still considered to be a transition element. Justify the statement.
  2. Why are {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{2+} / \mathrm{M}}^{\ominus}{/tex} values of Mn and Zn more negative than expected?
  3. Why do transition metals form alloys?
 3

OR
(b)

Answer the following questions on the basis of the figure given below:

  1. Which element in 3d series has lowest enthalpy of atomisation?
  2. Why do metals of the second and third series have greater enthalpies of atomisation?
  3. Why are enthalpies of atomisation of transition metals quite high?
 3
5.(a)
  1. Write the electronic configuration of d4 on the basis of crystal field splitting theory, if {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} < P.
  2. [Ni(CN)4]2- with square-planar structure is diamagnetic and [NiCl4]2- with tetrahedral geometry is paramagnetic. Give reason to support the statement.
    [Atomic number : Ni = 28]
  3. Write the number of ions produced in the solution from the following complex: [PtCl2(NH3)4]Cl2
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Calculate the spin only magnetic moment of the complex [FeF6]3-. (Atomic number of Fe = 26)
  2. Write the IUPAC name of the given complex: [Co(NH3)5Cl]Cl2
  3. Why is the complex [Co(en)3]3+ more stable than [CoF6]3-?
 3
6.(a)

Write equations involved in the following reactions:

  1. Ethanamine reacts with acetyl chloride.
  2. Aniline reacts with bromine water at room temperature.
  3. Aniline reacts with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide.
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Write the IUPAC name for the following organic compound: (CH3CH2)2NCH3
  2. Write the equations for the following:
    1. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis
    2. Hoffmann bromamide degradation
 3
7.(a)

Write reasons for the following:

  1. Ethylamine is soluble in water whereas aniline is insoluble.
  2. Amino group is o- and p-directing in aromatic electrophilic substitution reactions, but aniline on nitration gives a substantial amount of m-nitroaniline.
  3. Amines behave as nucleophiles.
 3

OR
(b)

How will you carry out the following conversions:

  1. Nitrobenzene to Aniline
  2. Ethanamide to Methanamine
  3. Ethanenitrile to Ethanamine
 3
8.

A compound A (C2H4O) on oxidation gives B (C2H4O2). A undergoes Iodoform reaction to give yellow precipitate and reacts with HCN to form the compound C. C on hydrolysis gives 2-hydroxypropanoic acid. Identify the compounds A, B and C. Write down equations for the reactions involved.

 3
9.
  1. Which ion amongst the following is colourless and why?
    Ti4+, Cr3+, V3+
    (Atomic number of Ti = 22, Cr = 24, V = 23)
  2. Why is Mn2+ much more resistant than Fe2+ towards oxidation?
  3. Highest oxidation state of a metal is shown in its oxide or fluoride only. Justify the statement.
 3
10.

Write the Nernst equation and calculate the emf of the following cell at 298 K:
Zn | Zn2+ (0·001 M) || H+ (0·01 M) | H2 (g) (1 bar) | Pt (s)
Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -0·76 V
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H_2}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = 0·00 V
[log 10 = 1]

 3
11.

Observe the given figure and answer the following questions:

  1. Write the expression for adsorption of gases on solids in the form of an equation.
  2. What is the slope of the graph?
  3. What does the intercept of the line represent?
 3

SECTION C
12.

Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow:

The rate law for a chemical reaction relates the reaction rate with the concentrations or partial pressures of the reactants. For a general reaction aA + bB {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C with no intermediate steps in its reaction mechanism, meaning that it is an elementary reaction, the rate law is given by r = k[A]x [B]y, where [A] and [B] express the concentrations of A and B in moles per litre. Exponents x and y vary for each reaction and are determined experimentally. The value of k varies with conditions that affect reaction rate, such as temperature, pressure, surface area, etc. The sum of these exponents is known as overall reaction order. A zero order reaction has a constant rate that is independent of the concentration of the reactants. A first order reaction depends on the concentration of only one reactant. A reaction is said to be second order when the overall order is two. Once we have determined the order of the reaction, we can go back and plug in one set of our initial values and solve for k.

 5
(i)

Calculate the overall order of a reaction which has the following rate expression:
Rate = k[A]1/2 [B]3/2

 1
(ii)

What is the effect of temperature on rate of reaction?

 1
(iii)

What is meant by rate of a reaction?

 1
(iv)(a)

A first order reaction takes 77·78 minutes for 50% completion. Calculate the time required for 30% completion of this reaction. (log 10 = 1, log 7 = 0·8450)

 2

OR
(b)

A first order reaction has a rate constant 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 per sec. How long will 5 g of this reactant take to reduce to 3 g? (log 3 = 0·4771; log 5 = 0·6990)

 2

answer_key_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/4/2 (set-2)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 11 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into three Sections - A, B and C.
  3. Section A - Questions no. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section B - Questions no. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section C - Question no. 12 is case based question, carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is not allowed.

SECTION : A
1.

In a plot of {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} against the square root of concentration (C1/2) for strong and weak electrolyte, the value of limiting molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte cannot be obtained graphically. Suggest a way to obtain this value. Also state the related law, if any.

Solution:

Limiting molar conductivity for weak electrolyte can be obtained by using Kohlrausch law.
It states that limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of conductivity of constituent ions.

 2
2.

Write reasons for the following statements:

  1. Benzoic acid does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  2. Oxidation of aldehydes is easier than that of ketones.
Solution:
  1. Benzoic acid do not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction because the carboxyl group is deactivating and the catalyst aluminium chloride gets bonded to the carboxyl group.
  2. Because of oxidation of aldehyde involves cleavage of C-H bond which is weaker than C-C bond of ketone.
 2
3.

Give reasons for the following statements: (Any two)

  1. Benzaldehyde is less reactive than propanal in nucleophilic addition reactions.
  2. Carboxylic acids do not give reactions of carbonyl group.
  3. 4-nitrobenzoic acid is a stronger acid than benzoic acid.
Solution:
  1. Carbon atom of carbonyl group of benzaldehyde is less electrophilic and polarity is reduced due to resonance.
  2. Because of resonance by -OH of COOH which reduces the electrophilicity of carboxyl carbon / Because of resonance in COOH group due to which carbon loses its carbonyl nature.
  3. Due to -I effect /electron withdrawing nature of NO2 group.
 2

SECTION : B
4.(a)
  1. Silver atom has completely filled d-orbitals in its ground state, it is still considered to be a transition element. Justify the statement.
  2. Why are {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{2+} / \mathrm{M}}^{\ominus}{/tex} values of Mn and Zn more negative than expected?
  3. Why do transition metals form alloys?
Solution:
  1. Silver has incompletely filled d-orbitals / d9 configuration in +2 oxidation state.
  2. Mn2+ and Zn2+ has stable half-filled and fully filled configurations / relatively less sublimation enthalpy of Mn and Zn.
  3. Because of similar atomic radii.
 3

OR
(b)

Answer the following questions on the basis of the figure given below:

  1. Which element in 3d series has lowest enthalpy of atomisation?
  2. Why do metals of the second and third series have greater enthalpies of atomisation?
  3. Why are enthalpies of atomisation of transition metals quite high?
Solution:
  1. Zn
  2. It is because of frequent metal-metal bonding / due to poor shielding effect of d and f orbitals.
  3. Due to strong intermetallic bonding / strong interatomic interaction.
 3
5.(a)
  1. Write the electronic configuration of d4 on the basis of crystal field splitting theory, if {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} < P.
  2. [Ni(CN)4]2- with square-planar structure is diamagnetic and [NiCl4]2- with tetrahedral geometry is paramagnetic. Give reason to support the statement.
    [Atomic number : Ni = 28]
  3. Write the number of ions produced in the solution from the following complex: [PtCl2(NH3)4]Cl2
Solution:
  1. {tex}t_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^3 e_{\mathrm{g}}^1{/tex}
  2. Ni2+ is 3d8 and has dsp2 hybridisation. Cyanide is a strong field ligand; electrons pair up so it is diamagnetic. In [NiCl4]2-, Cl- is a weak field ligand, electrons do not pair up, hence hybridisation is sp3. Thus, it is paramagnetic.
  3. 3
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Calculate the spin only magnetic moment of the complex [FeF6]3-. (Atomic number of Fe = 26)
  2. Write the IUPAC name of the given complex: [Co(NH3)5Cl]Cl2
  3. Why is the complex [Co(en)3]3+ more stable than [CoF6]3-?
Solution:
  1. Fe3+ -No. of unpaired electrons = 5
    {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{n(n+2)}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{5(5+2)}{/tex}
    = 5.916 BM
  2. Pentaamminechloridocobalt (III) chloride
  3. [Co(en)3]3+, chelate effect / formation of cyclic structure.
 3
6.(a)

Write equations involved in the following reactions:

  1. Ethanamine reacts with acetyl chloride.
  2. Aniline reacts with bromine water at room temperature.
  3. Aniline reacts with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide.
Solution:
  1. C2H5NH2 + CH3COCL {tex}\xrightarrow{Base}{/tex} C2H5NHCOCH3 + HCL
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Write the IUPAC name for the following organic compound: (CH3CH2)2NCH3
  2. Write the equations for the following:
    1. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis
    2. Hoffmann bromamide degradation
Solution:
  1. N-Ethyl-N-methylethanamine
    2. {tex}\overset{\mathrm O}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{NH}}_2}\;+\;{\mathrm{Br}}_2\;+\;4\mathrm{NaOH}{/tex}{tex}\rightarrow\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{NH}}_2\;+\;{\mathrm{NA}}_2{\mathrm{CO}}_3\;+\;2{\mathrm{Na}}_2\mathrm{Br}\;+\;2{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O{/tex}
 3
7.(a)

Write reasons for the following:

  1. Ethylamine is soluble in water whereas aniline is insoluble.
  2. Amino group is o- and p-directing in aromatic electrophilic substitution reactions, but aniline on nitration gives a substantial amount of m-nitroaniline.
  3. Amines behave as nucleophiles.
Solution:
  1. Ethylamine forms strong hydrogen bonds with water molecules whereas in aniline due to the large hydrocarbon part, the extent of H-bonding decreases.
  2. Because of protonation of aniline / formation of anilinium ion which deactivates the ring.
  3. Amines behaves as nucleophiles due to the presence of a lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom.
 3

OR
(b)

How will you carry out the following conversions:

  1. Nitrobenzene to Aniline
  2. Ethanamide to Methanamine
  3. Ethanenitrile to Ethanamine
Solution:
  2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CONH}}_2\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{Br}}_2/\mathrm{KOH}}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{NH}}_2{/tex}
  3. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2{\mathrm{NH}}_2{/tex}
 3
8.

A compound A (C2H4O) on oxidation gives B (C2H4O2). A undergoes Iodoform reaction to give yellow precipitate and reacts with HCN to form the compound C. C on hydrolysis gives 2-hydroxypropanoic acid. Identify the compounds A, B and C. Write down equations for the reactions involved.

Solution:

A = Acetaldehyde (CH3CHO)
B = Ethanoic acid (CH3COOH)
C = Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin (CH3CH(OH)CN)
{tex}\underset{\mathit(\mathit A\mathit)}{\mathit C{\mathit H}_{\mathit3}\mathit C\mathit H\mathit O}\mathit\;\overset{\mathit\lbrack\mathit O\mathit\rbrack}{\mathit\rightarrow}\mathit\;\underset{\mathit(\mathit B\mathit)}{\mathit C{\mathit H}_{\mathit3}\mathit C\mathit O\mathit O\mathit H}{/tex}
{tex}\underset{(\mathrm A)}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}}\;\xrightarrow{{\mathrm I}_2\;\mathrm{NAOH}}\;\underset{}{\underset{\mathrm{Yellow}\;\mathrm{ppt}}{{\mathrm{CHI}}_3}}{/tex}
{tex}\underset{(\mathrm A)}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}}\;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{HCN}}\;\underset{(\mathrm C)}{\underset{\mathrm{CN}}{\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OH}}}}​​​​​​​ {/tex}
{tex}\underset{(\mathrm C)}{\underset{\mathrm{CN}}{\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OH}}}}\;\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O/\mathrm H^+}\;\underset{}{\underset{\mathrm{COOH}}{\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OH}}}}{/tex}

 3
9.
  1. Which ion amongst the following is colourless and why?
    Ti4+, Cr3+, V3+
    (Atomic number of Ti = 22, Cr = 24, V = 23)
  2. Why is Mn2+ much more resistant than Fe2+ towards oxidation?
  3. Highest oxidation state of a metal is shown in its oxide or fluoride only. Justify the statement.
Solution:
  1. {tex}\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}{/tex}
    no unpaired electrons in d-orbital / no d-d transition / {tex}\mathrm{d}^0{/tex} configuration.
  2. Mn2+ is d5 and is more stable than Mn3+ (d4) whereas Fe3+(d5) is more stable than Fe2+(d4).
  3. Because fluorine and oxygen are highly electronegative / both are strong oxidising agents.
 3
10.

Write the Nernst equation and calculate the emf of the following cell at 298 K:
Zn | Zn2+ (0·001 M) || H+ (0·01 M) | H2 (g) (1 bar) | Pt (s)
Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -0·76 V
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H_2}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = 0·00 V
[log 10 = 1]

Solution:

{tex}E_{\text {cell }}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^0-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^2}{/tex}
{tex}E_{\text {cell }}^0{/tex} = 0.0 - (-0.76) = 0.76 V
= {tex}0.76-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{[0.001]}{[0.01]^2} {/tex}
= 0.76 - 0.0295 {tex}\times{/tex} 1
= 0.7305 V

 3
11.

Observe the given figure and answer the following questions:

  1. Write the expression for adsorption of gases on solids in the form of an equation.
  2. What is the slope of the graph?
  3. What does the intercept of the line represent?
Solution:

Chapter Delete from the Textbook.

 3

SECTION : C
12.

Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow:

The rate law for a chemical reaction relates the reaction rate with the concentrations or partial pressures of the reactants. For a general reaction aA + bB {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C with no intermediate steps in its reaction mechanism, meaning that it is an elementary reaction, the rate law is given by r = k[A]x [B]y, where [A] and [B] express the concentrations of A and B in moles per litre. Exponents x and y vary for each reaction and are determined experimentally. The value of k varies with conditions that affect reaction rate, such as temperature, pressure, surface area, etc. The sum of these exponents is known as overall reaction order. A zero order reaction has a constant rate that is independent of the concentration of the reactants. A first order reaction depends on the concentration of only one reactant. A reaction is said to be second order when the overall order is two. Once we have determined the order of the reaction, we can go back and plug in one set of our initial values and solve for k.

 5
(i)

Calculate the overall order of a reaction which has the following rate expression:
Rate = k[A]1/2 [B]3/2

Solution:

Order = {tex}\frac12{/tex} + {tex}\frac32{/tex} = 2

 1
(ii)

What is the effect of temperature on rate of reaction?

Solution:

The rate of a reaction increases with the increase in temperature.

 1
(iii)

What is meant by rate of a reaction?

Solution:

The change in concentration of the reactants or products per unit time.

 1
(iv)(a)

A first order reaction takes 77·78 minutes for 50% completion. Calculate the time required for 30% completion of this reaction. (log 10 = 1, log 7 = 0·8450)

Solution:

k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.693}{77.78}{/tex} = .008909 min-1
t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{a}{a-x}{/tex}
t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{.008909}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{100}{100-30}{/tex}
t = 258.5026 (log10 - log7)
= 258.5026 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.155
= 40.06 min or 40.02 min

 2

OR
(b)

A first order reaction has a rate constant 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 per sec. How long will 5 g of this reactant take to reduce to 3 g? (log 3 = 0·4771; log 5 = 0·6990)

Solution:

k = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{t}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{[R]_0}{[R]}{/tex}
t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{5}{3}{/tex}
t = 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex}103 [0.699 - 0.4771]
t = 511 s.

 2

CHEMISTRY (Theory) Time allowed:2 hours Maximum Marks:35 General Instructions : Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them: This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory. This question paper is divided into three Sections - A , B and C . Section A - Questions no. 1 to 3 are very short answer type questions, carrying 2 marks each. Section B - Questions no. 4 to 11 are short answer type questions, carrying 3 marks each. Section C - Question no. 12 is case based question, carrying 5 marks. Use of log tables and calculators is not allowed. SECTION : A 1. In a plot of {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex} against the square root of concentration (C 1/2 ) for strong and weak electrolyte, the value of limiting molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte cannot be obtained graphically. Suggest a way to obtain this value. Also state the related law, if any. Solution: Limiting molar conductivity for weak electrolyte can be obtained by using Kohlrausch law. It states that limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of conductivity of constituent ions. 2 2. Write reasons for the following statements: Benzoic acid does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Oxidation of aldehydes is easier than that of ketones. Solution: Benzoic acid do not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction because the carboxyl group is deactivating and the catalyst aluminium chloride gets bonded to the carboxyl group. Because of oxidation of aldehyde involves cleavage of C-H bond which is weaker than C-C bond of ketone. 2 3. Give reasons for the following statements: (Any two ) Benzaldehyde is less reactive than propanal in nucleophilic addition reactions. Carboxylic acids do not give reactions of carbonyl group. 4-nitrobenzoic acid is a stronger acid than benzoic acid. Solution: Carbon atom of carbonyl group of benzaldehyde is less electrophilic and polarity is reduced due to resonance. Because of resonance by -OH of COOH which reduces the electrophilicity of carboxyl carbon / Because of resonance in COOH group due to which carbon loses its carbonyl nature. Due to -I effect /electron withdrawing nature of NO 2 group. 2 SECTION : B 4. (a) Silver atom has completely filled d-orbitals in its ground state, it is still considered to be a transition element. Justify the statement. Why are {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{2+} / \mathrm{M}}^{\ominus}{/tex} values of Mn and Zn more negative than expected? Why do transition metals form alloys? Solution: Silver has incompletely filled d-orbitals / d 9 configuration in +2 oxidation state. Mn 2+ and Zn 2+ has stable half-filled and fully filled configurations / relatively less sublimation enthalpy of Mn and Zn. Because of similar atomic radii. 3 OR (b) Answer the following questions on the basis of the figure given below: Which element in 3d series has lowest enthalpy of atomisation? Why do metals of the second and third series have greater enthalpies of atomisation? Why are enthalpies of atomisation of transition metals quite high? Solution: Zn It is because of frequent metal-metal bonding / due to poor shielding effect of d and f orbitals. Due to strong intermetallic bonding / strong interatomic interaction. 3 5. (a) Write the electronic configuration of d 4 on the basis of crystal field splitting theory, if {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} < P. [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- with square-planar structure is diamagnetic and [NiCl 4 ] 2- with tetrahedral geometry is paramagnetic. Give reason to support the statement. [Atomic number : Ni = 28] Write the number of ions produced in the solution from the following complex: [PtCl 2 (NH 3 ) 4 ]Cl 2 Solution: {tex}t_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^3 e_{\mathrm{g}}^1{/tex} Ni 2+ is 3d 8 and has dsp 2 hybridisation. Cyanide is a strong field ligand; electrons pair up so it is diamagnetic. In [NiCl 4 ] 2- , Cl - is a weak field ligand, electrons do not pair up, hence hybridisation is sp 3 . Thus, it is paramagnetic. 3 3 OR (b) Calculate the spin only magnetic moment of the complex [FeF 6 ] 3- . (Atomic number of Fe = 26) Write the IUPAC name of the given complex: [Co(NH 3 ) 5 Cl]Cl 2 Why is the complex [Co(en) 3 ] 3+ more stable than [CoF 6 ] 3- ? Solution: Fe 3+ -No. of unpaired electrons = 5 {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{n(n+2)}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{5(5+2)}{/tex} = 5.916 BM Pentaamminechloridocobalt (III) chloride [Co(en) 3 ] 3+ , chelate effect / formation of cyclic structure. 3 6. (a) Write equations involved in the following reactions: Ethanamine reacts with acetyl chloride. Aniline reacts with bromine water at room temperature. Aniline reacts with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide. Solution: C 2 H 5 NH 2 + CH 3 COCL {tex}\xrightarrow{Base}{/tex} C 2 H 5 NHCOCH 3 + HCL 3 OR (b) Write the IUPAC name for the following organic compound: (CH 3 CH 2 ) 2 NCH 3 Write the equations for the following: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis Hoffmann bromamide degradation Solution: N-Ethyl-N-methylethanamine {tex}\overset{\mathrm O}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{NH}}_2}\;+\;{\mathrm{Br}}_2\;+\;4\mathrm{NaOH}{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{NH}}_2\;+\;{\mathrm{NA}}_2{\mathrm{CO}}_3\;+\;2{\mathrm{Na}}_2\mathrm{Br}\;+\;2{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O{/tex} 3 7. (a) Write reasons for the following: Ethylamine is soluble in water whereas aniline is insoluble. Amino group is o- and p-directing in aromatic electrophilic substitution reactions, but aniline on nitration gives a substantial amount of m-nitroaniline. Amines behave as nucleophiles. Solution: Ethylamine forms strong hydrogen bonds with water molecules whereas in aniline due to the large hydrocarbon part, the extent of H-bonding decreases. Because of protonation of aniline / formation of anilinium ion which deactivates the ring. Amines behaves as nucleophiles due to the presence of a lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom. 3 OR (b) How will you carry out the following conversions: Nitrobenzene to Aniline Ethanamide to Methanamine Ethanenitrile to Ethanamine Solution: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CONH}}_2\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{Br}}_2/\mathrm{KOH}}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{NH}}_2{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2{\mathrm{NH}}_2{/tex} 3 8. A compound A (C 2 H 4 O) on oxidation gives B (C 2 H 4 O 2 ). A undergoes Iodoform reaction to give yellow precipitate and reacts with HCN to form the compound C . C on hydrolysis gives 2-hydroxypropanoic acid. Identify the compounds A , B and C . Write down equations for the reactions involved. Solution: A = Acetaldehyde (CH 3 CHO) B = Ethanoic acid (CH 3 COOH) C = Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin (CH 3 CH(OH)CN) {tex}\underset{\mathit(\mathit A\mathit)}{\mathit C{\mathit H}_{\mathit3}\mathit C\mathit H\mathit O}\mathit\;\overset{\mathit\lbrack\mathit O\mathit\rbrack}{\mathit\rightarrow}\mathit\;\underset{\mathit(\mathit B\mathit)}{\mathit C{\mathit H}_{\mathit3}\mathit C\mathit O\mathit O\mathit H}{/tex} {tex}\underset{(\mathrm A)}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}}\;\xrightarrow{{\mathrm I}_2\;\mathrm{NAOH}}\;\underset{}{\underset{\mathrm{Yellow}\;\mathrm{ppt}}{{\mathrm{CHI}}_3}}{/tex} {tex}\underset{(\mathrm A)}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}}\;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{HCN}}\;\underset{(\mathrm C)}{\underset{\mathrm{CN}}{\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OH}}}}​​​​​​​ {/tex} {tex}\underset{(\mathrm C)}{\underset{\mathrm{CN}}{\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OH}}}}\;\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O/\mathrm H^+}\;\underset{}{\underset{\mathrm{COOH}}{\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OH}}}}{/tex} 3 9. Which ion amongst the following is colourless and why? Ti 4+ , Cr 3+ , V 3+ (Atomic number of Ti = 22, Cr = 24, V = 23) Why is Mn 2+ much more resistant than Fe 2+ towards oxidation? Highest oxidation state of a metal is shown in its oxide or fluoride only. Justify the statement. Solution: {tex}\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}{/tex} no unpaired electrons in d-orbital / no d-d transition / {tex}\mathrm{d}^0{/tex} configuration. Mn 2+ is d 5 and is more stable than Mn 3+ (d 4 ) whereas Fe 3+ (d 5 ) is more stable than Fe 2+ (d 4 ). Because fluorine and oxygen are highly electronegative / both are strong oxidising agents. 3 10. Write the Nernst equation and calculate the emf of the following cell at 298 K: Zn | Zn 2+ (0·001 M) || H + (0·01 M) | H 2 (g) (1 bar) | Pt (s) Given : {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = -0·76 V {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H_2}}^{\ominus}{/tex} = 0·00 V [log 10 = 1] Solution: {tex}E_{\text {cell }}{/tex} = {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^0-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^2}{/tex} {tex}E_{\text {cell }}^0{/tex} = 0.0 - (-0.76) = 0.76 V = {tex}0.76-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{[0.001]}{[0.01]^2} {/tex} = 0.76 - 0.0295 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 0.7305 V 3 11. What does the intercept of the line represent? Solution: Chapter Delete from the Textbook. 3 SECTION : C 12. Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow: The rate law for a chemical reaction relates the reaction rate with the concentrations or partial pressures of the reactants. For a general reaction aA + bB {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C with no intermediate steps in its reaction mechanism, meaning that it is an elementary reaction, the rate law is given by r = k[A] x [B] y , where [A] and [B] express the concentrations of A and B in moles per litre. Exponents x and y vary for each reaction and are determined experimentally. The value of k varies with conditions that affect reaction rate, such as temperature, pressure, surface area, etc. The sum of these exponents is known as overall reaction order. A zero order reaction has a constant rate that is independent of the concentration of the reactants. A first order reaction depends on the concentration of only one reactant. A reaction is said to be second order when the overall order is two. Once we have determined the order of the reaction, we can go back and plug in one set of our initial values and solve for k. 5 (i) Calculate the overall order of a reaction which has the following rate expression: Rate = k[A] 1/2 [B] 3/2 Solution: Order = {tex}\frac12{/tex} + {tex}\frac32{/tex} = 2 1 (ii) What is the effect of temperature on rate of reaction? Solution: The rate of a reaction increases with the increase in temperature. 1 (iii) What is meant by rate of a reaction? Solution: The change in concentration of the reactants or products per unit time. 1 (iv) (a) A first order reaction takes 77·78 minutes for 50% completion. Calculate the time required for 30% completion of this reaction. (log 10 = 1, log 7 = 0·8450) Solution: k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_{\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.693}{77.78}{/tex} = .008909 min -1 t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{.008909}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{100}{100-30}{/tex} t = 258.5026 (log10 - log7) = 258.5026 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.155 = 40.06 min or 40.02 min 2 OR (b) A first order reaction has a rate constant 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 per sec. How long will 5 g of this reactant take to reduce to 3 g? (log 3 = 0·4771; log 5 = 0·6990) Solution: k = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{t}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{[R]_0}{[R]}{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{2 \cdot 303}{1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{5}{3}{/tex} t = 2.303 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 [0.699 - 0.4771] t = 511 s. 2
